grammar.usage: Actions that happened before another action or time in the past
grammar.example: He had gone out when I came into the house.

grammar.usage: Actions continuing up to a point in the past, emphasizing duration or cause
grammar.example: It had been raining very hard for two hours before it stopped.

blocks.tableName: S + had + past participle (= V3 / V(ed))

rows.subject: S + had + V3/ed
rows.example: EX: He had gone out when I came into the house.

rows.subject: S + had + not + V3/ed
rows.example: EX: She hadn't come home when I got there.

rows.subject: Had + S + V3/ed?
rows.example: EX: Had the film started when you arrived at the cinema?

blocks.content: 2. HOW TO CHANGE VERBS INTO THE PAST
2.1. REGULAR VERBS: ADD ED (Refer to Unit 2)

bullets:
a) Add ed to the end of the verb:
EX: watch → watched; turn → turned; want → wanted; attach → attached

b) If the verb ends with "e", just add "d":
EX: type → typed; smile → smiled; agree → agreed

c) If the verb has ONE syllable, ends with ONE consonant, and has ONE vowel before the consonant, double the last consonant and add "-ed":
EX: stop → stopped; shop → shopped; tap → tapped
* Note: Some words don't follow this rule: commit → committed; travel → travelled; prefer → preferred

d) Verbs ending with "y":
- If "y" is preceded by a vowel (e, u, o, a, i), keep "y" and add ed. EX: enjoy → enjoyed; play → played; stay → stayed
- If "y" is preceded by a consonant, change "y" to "i" and add ed. EX: carry → carried; study → studied; hurry → hurried

rows: move from one place to another
continue to have or hold something
notice a scent with the nose
make words on paper or a screen
carry or support with your hands

bullets:
To learn and look up irregular verbs (V2/V3) quickly, you can open the online lookup table here:
- [List of irregular verbs](https://cdn.thuvienphapluat.vn/phap-luat/2022-2/NHPT/bang-360-dong-tu-bat-quy-tac-tvpl.pdf)
- [Oxford Dictionary](https://www.oxfordlearnersdictionaries.com/)

a) Use Past Perfect simple in case of 2 actions happened in the past, we use past perfect simple for the action that happened before and past simple for the action that happened after.
EX: I met her after she had divorced.

b) Past perfect simple is used to express an action that happened and was completed before a certain time in the past, or before another action that also ended in the past.
EX: We had had lunch when she arrived.

c) Action happens as a prerequisite for another action.
EX: I had prepared for the exams and was ready to do well.

d) In conditional sentences type 3 to express a unreal condition.
EX: She would have come to the party if she had been invited.

e) Action happens in a period of time in the past, before another time in the past.
EX: I had lived abroad for twenty years when I received the transfer.

items.label: Signal words
bullets:
a) In the sentence appears words such as:
- By: I'd finished my homework by 9 o'clock.
- By the time: By the time I got the class, the lesson had started.
- Before: The teacher had checked the answers before the lesson.
- After: I left after I'd finished the test.
- Just: Simon had just finished the test when the bell rang.
- When: I left when I'd finished the test.
b) Depends on the meaning.

items.label: Notes
bullets:
Using the simple past or the past perfect can change the meaning of a sentence.
- EX: The lesson started when I arrived.
  → The lesson started when I arrived.
- EX: The lesson had started when I arrived.
  → The lesson had started when I arrived.

rows: → Express an action that happened in the past and continues to the present or an action that happened at an unspecified time in the past.
→ Express an action that happened and continued up to a certain time in the past (the time in the past is specified).

rows.subject: S + had + been + V-ing
rows.example: EX: It had been raining very hard for two hours before it stopped.

rows.subject: S + had + not + been + V-ing
rows.example: EX: My father hadn't been doing anything when my mother came home.

rows.subject: Had + S + been + V-ing?
rows.example: EX: Had she been watching TV for 4 hours before she went to eat dinner?

bullets:
a) Express an action that was happening before another action in the past (emphasizing continuity).
EX: I had been thinking about that before you mentioned it.

b) Express an action that was the cause of something in the past.
EX: Sam gained weight (=result) because she had been overeating (=reason).

c) Express an action that happened to prepare for another action.
EX: I had been practicing for five months and was ready for the championship.

d) Express an action that happened continuously before another action in the past.
EX: She had been walking for 2 hours before 11 p.m. last night.
EX: My younger brother had been crying for almost 1 hour before lunchtime.

e) Emphasize an action that left a result in the past.
EX: Yesterday morning, he was exhausted because he had been working on his report all night.
EX: She would have been more confident if she had been preparing better.

items.label: Signal words
bullets:
a) In the sentence appears words such as:
- until then
- by then
- prior to that time
- before, after
- all day, all night...
b) Depends on the meaning.

vocabulary.example: I have to finish my homework before I can play video games.

vocabulary.meaning: rules for forming words and sentences
vocabulary.example: English grammar can be difficult to master.
She has a terrible headache and needs to rest.

vocabulary.meaning: work done to clean and care for a house
vocabulary.example: My brother and I help our parents with the housework.
The streets were flooded after the heavy rain.
The two cars crashed at the intersection.
We had a fantastic time at the concert last night.
He was nervous about speaking in front of the class.

vocabulary.meaning: read words aloud so people can hear
vocabulary.example: The teacher asked me to read out the answers.
Put the letter in the envelope and mail it.

vocabulary.meaning: study of the Earth's surface and countries
vocabulary.example: We are learning about the rivers of Asia in geography class.

vocabulary.meaning: cry out loudly because of happiness
vocabulary.example: The children shouted with joy when they heard the good news.
